data.title: VTEC: Difficult To Work Out Who This Is Suitable For
data.description:
Horses for courses

As I've been known to point out, much of economics - not all, but much - is simply the encoding of folk wisdom. Possibly made a little precise so that it can be tested, definitely put into more obscure and obfuscating language so that it can be charged for.

Some of that is also true about finance and financials, as here. The obvious point we have to grasp is that horses for courses. There are varied instruments out there, each with a different design. That design dictates how they work, fairly obviously. But that also means that we need to grasp those basics so that we use them for the appropriate purpose.

If we don't grasp the design, then we'll quite possibly use the instrument to try to do something it just won't. That would not be good for whatever it was that we were trying to achieve by that use, obviously. But it also opens us up to the possibility of losing substantial sums of our hard-earned money, which is rather worse.

Just as an example, and to be extreme, playing in futures and options is not the way to gain a risk free and consistent stream of income. Nor are Treasury bills likely to provide us with a large capital gain. On the other hand, Treasuries and consistent and risk free incomes are perfectly consonant, as are large capital gains - and losses - in options and futures.

Municipal bonds

Munis - municipal - are the third grand class of bonds in the US markets. Treasuries - The Feds - corporates and munis. Treasuries are "risk free," which they aren't. But they are credit risk free - which they're not really either, for governments can and will go bust. It's just that if the Feds go bust, we've all got much greater problems than the value of any financial savings we might have once had. So we consider them credit risk free concentrating upon inflation and interest rate risk.

Corporate bonds, obviously, companies can go bust. So can municipalities. So, in this sense, corporate and munis belong together. The implication of their credit risk is that if we decide we want to play in those markets, then we need to be diversified.

One problem with this is that bond markets are pretty thin. Not much trading of secondary bonds out there - the average bond, so I'm told, trades twice, at issue and redemption. Spreads on smaller issues can be pretty wide. We can indeed build a diversified portfolio, but it'll be by buying at issue and holding until that redemption. Just an example, but large areas of the European corporate bond market operate in $200k minimum par lot sizes. That's a bit chunky for near all private or retail investors like us.

So, the method of gaining that diversification across credit risks it to buy into a fund. Could be an ETF, or a closed end fund, there are other options as well. An open-ended fund will rarely trade far off net asset value, a closed end fund can wander quite a long way from it. On the other hand, a closed end fund cannot be subject to a run in the same way an open-ended can.

The problem with open-ended funds is the potential liquidity of the underlying. If lots of people wish to redeem, then the fund must sell, obviously. But if the underlying they're trying to sell is illiquid, then they'll have difficulty doing so, or must do so at distress prices. This increases the incentive to cash out, raises redemptions, and it becomes a run.

In something liquid like Treasuries or major stocks, this isn't a problem. In small bond issues, private companies, or real estate, it can be. Several funds over the years in those last two have fallen over as a result of this problem.

So, if we want to be in munis we really want to be in a fund of some kind. Unless we're very much larger than the average investor, that's really the way we'll gain the diversification we need in the face of credit risk.

Munis and tax

The big issue with municipal bonds is that they're usually (not always!) free of the Federal income tax. Further, instate issuers are usually free of the state income tax for instate holders of those bonds. NAN is just such a fund aimed at New York State residents, for example.

The effect of this is as an economist would predict. Munis pay lower interest rates (more accurately, coupons) than their credit risk a time tenor would usually indicate. Lower than Treasuries, even. We can see the effect of the tax exemption here:

That's the average across all issues. It's also the effect of taxes on the interest rates munis have to pay. If the interest is taxable, then they pay 1.6% more than if it isn't. Logically, investors are buying taking account of tenor, credit risk and so on, and so the tax exemption is worth that much to them.

Yes, true, economists claim a little too much that markets clear and so on, but here they're about right. The after tax return to munis that aren't taxed should be, given consistent risk and time span, the same as taxable ones. And more detailed research tends to find that this is true, the coupons on munis are pushed down by the tax benefits received by those who buy them.

Vanguard California Tax-Exempt Bond ETF (BATS:VTEC)

So, funds which hold tax-exempt bonds - what we call munis most of the time - make sense for those who pay tax. They make more sense for those who pay higher rates of tax - recall, above, the coupon is determined by the *average* tax bill of buyers, so if you're a higher than average taxpayer you get a greater than market benefit. We want a fund because diversification of credit risk.

In states where there's a high income tax chargeable at that state level, it might well be worth looking into ways to alleviate that burden, as well as the Federal one. So, here we are, a state-based muni bond fund. Carrying CA issues and clearly designed for CA residents. Because munis are only free of state income tax instate and for instate taxpayers.

So, in considering VTEC, if you're not a California resident, then this is of no interest to you. If you're not a Federal taxpayer, this is also of no interest to you. For income non-taxpayers in California, this is just a horse not for your course.

Tax equivalent yield

The standard way of calculating this all out is to look at these tax-privileged investments (and we can do it with any tax-privilege we like) and their returns, then work out what you'd have to get as a non-tax-privileged yield to match it. The tax system is complicated, many different rates and all that, but that's the core of it. Fortunately, people publish tables of this stuff, like this one for CA residents:

As we can see, the tax break is worth more and more to the investor as income - and so marginal tax rate - rises. A 5% yield is really worth 10.9% to someone at the very tippy top of the income/tax range. In the sense that the cash in hand gained from a 5% untaxable is the same as a 10.9% taxed.

Now, note something interesting about this. At low tax-free yields the tax-free investment isn't worth it - because taking the taxable yield, paying taxes, and grumbling about it produces more cash in hand. And this is where, as far as I'm concerned, VTEC has a problem.

VTEC

There's this:

That yield there is 2.9%. OK, so running that up to the tax equivalent yield - note the already included CA income tax - means that we need to find a taxable yield of 4.5% if we're in the 100k to 200k income range or 6.5% if we're over 1 million already.

Well, OK, we can get 4.77% in short-term Treasuries - so no credit nor interest rate risk - in USFR. Or even 6.2% in MQY, which is already free of Federal income tax, even if the CA one still has to be paid.

The whole and entire point - what makes them attractive - of muni bonds is that the tax-free yield equals or beats what we can get in taxable yields and then pay tax upon. A muni bond fund that doesn't achieve that isn't, to my mind, working at the task it's been set to do.

We can beat VTEC's income by being in less risky (so Treasuries) or equally risky (a national muni fund). So, VTEC doesn't work.

The usual financial details

And then fund facts:

And fund holdings:

Please note that I'm not saying there's something wrong here. Vanguard is charging very little for this (0.08%) but that's where I think the problem actually lies.

This isn't an "index fund" even though it is, in fact, benchmarked against an index of CA muni bonds. Not in the sense that we think of the indices we actually know, S&P 500 and so on. It doesn't even hold all the issues which do make up the index is it benchmarked against.

On the other hand, it is, sorta, an index fund in that it holds a vast swathe of positions and does not select (at least not very much) between them. It's deliberately holding - and this is why the fee is 0.08% - "the market" and not being selective.

Why I'm wrong

Now, of course, there could be something I've missed here. But I've looked through and seen that the same general risk (so, medium term munis) funds offer great cash in hand return. Even with the CA tax to pay. For everyone except the top tax rate payers, even short-term Treasuries look similar in end-return.

I agree, because I always do, that I could be wrong. If I am doing, tell me as well - that's how I learn.

But I'm not wrong in the yield on this fund, nor that better is available elsewhere at what seems to be similar risk. This is true even if we move from published yield to SEC 30 day and so on.

My opinion

Municipal bonds are Federal income tax free. Instate ones are, to instate taxpayers, state income tax free. Therefore, in states with high income taxes, there are muni bond funds that cater to that market.

But the entire point is to end up with a better final return than investing for yield and then paying the taxes. This is a test that - as far as I can see - VTEC fails. Until about halfway up the income distribution, it's possible to do better than VTEC by being in simply floating rate Treasuries (USFR). And it's possible to better in a managed muni - rather than index - national fund and then pay state taxes (MQY).

It's possible that at the very tippy top of the tax and income distribution this changes, but I'm still not seeing it.

I think the reason for this is that VTEC holds "the market" rather than optimising for the income through managed holdings.

As I said at the top, the design of an instrument is important, this tells us who it is good for, what purpose it is good for, what problem it solves. I'm not seeing that VTEC successfully solves the problem.

Do something else.

The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit hartfordfunds.com.

Share Class Inception: A, R3, R4, R5, Y - 6/30/08; F - 2/28/17; R6 - 2/28/18. Performance shown prior to the inception of a class reflects performance and operating expenses of another class(es) (excluding sales charges, if applicable). Had fees and expenses of a class been reflected for the periods prior to the inception of that class, performance would be different. Since inception (SI) performance is from 6/30/08. Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge. Performance for periods of less than one year is not annualized.

Benchmark: MSCI ACWI ex USA Index is a broad-based, unmanaged, market capitalization weighted, total return index that measures the performance of both developed and emerging stock markets, excluding the US MSCI index performance is shown net of dividend withholding tax. Indices are unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

Percentage Of Portfolio

Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

International equities rose in the first quarter. Financial markets were rattled by seismic shifts in US foreign policy, which significantly altered the outlook for global trade, economic growth, and inflation. Enthusiasm for AI-related expansion and a more accommodative US business environment waned in response to rising economic and tariff uncertainty. Consequently, capital rotated away from the US to international markets, particularly Europe, which significantly outperformed amid signs of a more favorable economic backdrop, including less restrictive monetary policy, improving leading indicators such as manufacturing output, and greater fiscal stimulus.

Performance Summary

Hartford International Equity Fund (I Share) (6.01%) outperformed the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index (Net) (5.23%) during the quarter.

Security selection drove relative outperformance, as strong selection in the financials and industrials sectors was partially offset by weaker selection in the consumer discretionary and communication services sectors.

Sector allocation, a result of the portfolio’s bottom-up stock-selection process, detracted from relative results. The negative effects from our overweight exposure to information technology and underweight exposure to financials were partially offset by the positive impact from our overweight exposure to communication services.

Factor impact on the Fund was negative, driven by its smaller cap footprint and underweight to stocks with more financial leverage.

From a top-down perspective, regional exposures (country and currency) were relatively flat, as the negative impacts from the Fund’s positioning in Europe and Emerging Markets were offset by exposure to names in Japan, which contributed.

The top relative contributors to performance during the quarter included our positions in Rheinmetall (industrials) and Prudential (financials).

Positioning & Outlook

Increased volatility and signs of a shift away from US exceptionalism defined equity markets as investors navigated the impact of new US tariffs and the Trump administration's uncertain agenda. Consequently, this turbulence resulted in a breadth of international opportunities as the US momentum trade reversed and foreign economies experienced various tailwinds, including increased fiscal spending and quantitative easing. We believe this increased market volatility and potential global regime shift sets up fundamental stock pickers for success as they differentiate between stocks that can navigate these geopolitical uncertainties and those that cannot. As ever, we remain focused on balancing risks in the portfolio and structurally allocate across different managers and styles to provide diversified opportunities in various market areas.

At the end of the period, our largest overweights were to the communication services and information technology sectors, while our largest underweights were to the financials and utilities sectors, relative to the benchmark.

1Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

Important Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Security prices fluctuate in value depending on general market and economic conditions and the prospects of individual companies. • Foreign investments may be more volatile and less liquid than U.S. investments and are subject to the risk of currency fluctuations and adverse political, economic and regulatory developments. These risks may be greater, and include additional risks, for investments in emerging markets or if the Fund focuses in a particular geographic region or country. • Small- and mid-cap securities can have greater risks and volatility than large-cap securities. • The securities of large market capitalization companies may underperform other segments of the market. • The Fund's strategy for allocating assets among portfolio management teams may not work as intended. • The value of securities selected using quantitative analysis can perform differently from the market as a whole or from their expected performance.

Investors should carefully consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. This and other important information is contained in a fund's full prospectus and summary prospectus, which can be obtained by visiting hartfordfunds.com. Please read it carefully before investing.

Mutual funds are distributed by Hartford Funds Distributors, LLC (HFD), Member FINRA. Advisory services are provided by Hartford Funds Management Company, LLC (HFMC). Certain funds are sub-advised by Wellington Management Company LLP. HFMC and Wellington Management are SEC registered investment advisers. HFD and HFMC are not affiliated with any sub-adviser.

NOT FDIC INSURED • MAY LOSE VALUE • NO BANK GUARANTEE

data.title: NatWest Group: A Double Beat To Start The Year
data.description:
For all the uncertainty out there, 2025 is still shaping up to be a good year for NatWest Group (NYSE:NWG). On the business front, first quarter results could hardly have gone better, with the bank cruising past consensus on the way to an exceptional 18.5% return on tangible equity. As for the stock, well, things have gone just as well. Aided by a weaker U.S. dollar, NatWest's ADSs have returned another ~20% since my last update in February, bringing their year-to-date return up to the 30% mark.

This strong run leaves the stock trading for almost 1.4x tangible book value. In past years, I would have considered that nosebleed territory for a European bank. Today? I am not so sure, with NatWest still enjoying a multi-year tailwind from hedge income. While the potential for multiple expansion is more limited from here, the strength of NatWest's earnings still justifies its current valuation. Overall, I remain bullish.

A Double Beat To Start The Year

NatWest reported its first quarter financials last week. Results were about as good as they could have been, with the bank comfortably beating consensus on both revenue and earnings.

As my regular readers will know, NatWest's interest rate hedging program has been the key component of my bullish case. Swaps written when interest rates were very low continue to mature, and the associated pick-up in yield is powering both margin expansion and growth in net interest income. Being a hedge, this trend is running counter-cyclical to wider U.K. interest rates now that the Bank of England is in easing mode.

This continues to play out quite nicely. Last quarter, NatWest reported total income of £3.98 billion, beating consensus by around 4%. Within that, net interest income was £3.03 billion, beating by roughly £40 million. Earning asset balances were basically in line, but the bank's margin came in better than expected. NatWest's net interest margin was 2.27% last quarter, up 22bps year-on-year, 8bps quarter-on-quarter and around 4bps ahead of consensus. Per below, hedge dynamics are set to be a major tailwind here over the next couple of years, and this will power further gains in NIM and NII.

Non-interest income was also strong this quarter, rising by nearly 16% year-on-year to £954 million. The bank's markets business was particularly buoyant, with elevated volatility contributing to strong growth in trading income, while underwriting income from capital markets was also solid. Following this performance, management tightened full-year revenue guidance toward the high-end of its previous range (£15.2-£15.7 billion). Given trade tensions and the threat to economic growth, I could see potential for weakness in certain areas like capital markets, but that shouldn't matter much: the strength of last quarter's revenue print combined with ongoing hedge tailwinds make £15.7 billion comfortably doable, if not outright conservative.

Turning to costs, operating expenses of £1.98 billion beat by around 6%. Credit charges came in slightly weaker, missing by over 10%, but this is a difficult line to forecast accurately quarter-to-quarter. More importantly, asset quality continues to look solid, with Stage 3 (i.e. non-performing) loan balances ticking up just 10bps quarter-on-quarter relative to total loans. At 1.5%, this remains around 20bps below NatWest's recent historical average. Balances of early-stage delinquencies (loans less than 30-days past due) also remain flat.

Of course, most of the recent tariff-related volatility only came in the current quarter. We will only begin to see the full impact in later periods. With that in mind, NatWest looks prudently provisioned. Its current expected credit loss allowance currently stands at around £3.5 billion, and this includes around £300 million in post-model overlays. Under its Base Case scenario, the bank would actually release a modest number of provisions. While it would need to raise more in a more severe downturn, it is also earning around £7.5 billion in annual pre-provision income to absorb losses. Accordingly, credit isn't a major worry of mine.

Weighing all that up, NatWest reported an attributable profit of £1.25 billion last quarter. That was around 18% ahead of consensus, with the associated return on tangible equity (18.5%) beating by 270bps.

The Valuation

As management noted on the call, there was some lumpiness around costs and non-interest income that basically went the bank's way last quarter. That means we shouldn't be too quick to extrapolate this performance over the rest of the year.

Having said that, let's place the bank's earnings into some context. The shares trade for £4.76 each as I type ($12.85 per ADS), equivalent to 1.37x NatWest's tangible net asset value ('TNAV') at the end of last quarter. An 18.5% return on tangible equity would equate to a price-earnings ratio of just 7.4x. As you can see, shareholders can afford to see quite a bit of slippage before these shares trade for over 10x earnings.

The sell-side analysts that cover this bank don't expect that to happen. Consensus has ROTE landing in the 16%-17% area throughout the 2025-2027 period, which would keep the stock's P/E multiple in the single-digit area.

That basically explains why I remain so bullish here. Furthermore, because NatWest's earnings are so strong, shareholders are seeing strong growth in TNAV per share while also receiving a nice dividend. For example, last quarter's TNAV of £3.47 per share represented year-on-year growth of roughly 15%. NatWest also paid out £0.215 per share in cash dividends in that time, equal to a 4.5% trailing yield.

There's another element powering the bank's balance sheet growth: the unwind of the cash flow hedge reserve. Just as U.S. banks have seen unrealized losses on bonds depress their book values, the same is true of NatWest's interest rate swaps. This will fade as the hedge continues to be repriced over the next couple of years, contributing to TNAV growth. Between balance sheet growth, a flat TNAV multiple and dividends, these shares continue to offer today's buyer the potential for double-digit annualized returns.

Conclusion

NatWest begins its fiscal 2025 in great shape, with first quarter results comfortably exceeding expectations. Lumpiness around costs and non-interest income mean that last quarter's 18.5% ROTE is probably a high-water mark rather than a base, but the valuation hardly requires the bank to maintain that level of profitability. Plus, growing hedge income is going to keep its earnings at a very healthy level regardless. Since this is likely to keep the stock's P/E ratio in the single-digit region, I remain bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PepsiCo Poised For Market-Beating Returns
data.description:
PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) (NEOE:PEP:CA) is a global brand powerhouse with a storied history. Overt time, the business's economics have proven incredibly appealing, providing investors with attractive returns for a long period of time. However, the company's stock has run into some headwinds of late, and there are a number of explanations out there purportedly accounting for PEP's difficulties. Ultimately, however, I think these explanations are misleading investors. On the contrary, I think that PEP is currently trading at significantly attractive prices (~$131 at the time of writing), which should provide good returns for investors going forward.

General Business Overview

Quite obviously, everyone has heard of Pepsi’s flagship carbonated soft drink, Pepsi Cola. However, newcomers to the Pepsi business are likely often surprised at how relatively small of a percentage the legacy cola business contributes to the overall enterprise. The company describes its business lines as follows:

In a recent investor presentation, they present their brand portfolio in this way:

Here is how sales across divisions stacked up in 2024:

And here are how operating profits across divisions fared during the same time:

Furthermore, these trends have persisted over time. Here are sales numbers over the past ten years for the 4 largest divisions:

… and here are operating profits over the same time frame:

So, with this by way of background, we can already note several key points that offer a more accurate summary of Pepsi’s business which would augment the likely inaccurate stereotype of the company someone completely new to the business might have:

  • The company’s sales are a roughly 60% - vs. - 40% split United States - vs. - Rest of the world
  • While the beverage business makes up a majority of revenues, it is actually the salty snacks and convenience foods business that drives profitability.
  • While certain brands (e.g., Pepsi and Lay’s) might make up an over-representation of sales, one should think of the company in terms of an extensive brand portfolio as opposed to simply two or three brands.

Current State of Pepsi’s Stock

So, with that, by way of background of the company, let’s fast-forward to present-day and see what’s going on with the stock. Here is the comparative chart for the total return of PEP vs. the S&P 500 over the past 10 years:

PEP has mostly performed in-line with the S&P500 over this period. Notably, though, performance over the past year or so has been particularly bad. I’ve seen a number of candidate explanations for this trend, some of which include the following:

  • A more health-conscious consumer that is opting out of Pepsi’s core brands
  • A greater trend in usage of GLP-1’s indirectly creating negative demand pressure
  • Product recalls, especially in the Frito-Lay and Quaker Foods divisions
  • RFK Jr.'s influence as head of the Department of Health and Human Services (HSS) and the influences policies he is spearheading would have on companies like Pepsi
  • Future expectations regarding further margin pressures from tariffs and increased supply chain costs

And this is not an exhaustive list, although it largely accounts for the general vibes that seem to be out there. In my opinion, though, this situation calls to mind the old adage that “news follows the stock price” – i.e., if you need an explanation for a stock’s price going up, you can find one, and if you need an explanation for a stock’s price going down, you can find one as well.

In any case, for dividend aficionados out there, and for value investors more generally, this state of affairs has made Pepsi's stock begin to look pretty attractive. For anyone who doesn’t know, Pepsi is a dividend king – i.e., a stock which has increased its dividend for the last 50 years+.

And with the stock having come down so much of late, new investors can buy in at a historically low starting dividend yield – 4.11% at the time of writing.

My Assessment

So, what do I think of the current opportunity with Pepsi’s stock? Well, first of all, as I’ve already mentioned, I’m very suspicious of the stereotypical explanations that are out there. However, beyond simply hand-waving, let’s engage some of these more in-depth.

RFK Jr. at HHS

Regarding this narrative, this chart is especially telling:

I’ve drawn a red-arrow pointing to the day that RFK Jr. was picked by Trump to head HHS. The stock price closed on that day (11/14/24) at $165.15 and hasn’t regained that level since. Thus, on its face, this would lend credence to the idea that RFK Jr. (and his preferred policies) is having an out-sized influence on the prospects (in investors' minds, at least) of a company like Pepsi.

However, to this, I would say that this is very welcome news to new Pepsi investors, if, in fact, RFK Jr.'s ascendancy at HHS is having the sort of impact on PEP's stock price as the above chart might suggest. I think that history has borne out the idea that, generally speaking, political figures (especially American political figures) turn out to be a lot less efficacious than people imagine they will be beforehand. Let's game out a few possibilities.

First, RFK Jr. is very much against artificial food dyes. The science on this, however, is highly controversial, as high-lighted here. This makes it more likely that litigation by companies like Pepsi might be able to stymy such regulatory efforts, if not fully derail them altogether. What's more, though, Pepsi has already vowed to phase out artificial dyes from many of its products by year-end, in any case, and, to my mind, it's not at all clear that doing so is going to massively affect the over-arching economics of their business.

Secondly, as discussed here, RFK Jr. is very much against high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS), and there has been discussion that he might seek to remove government subsidies that lower the price of HFCS. However, as the just-referenced article notes, it's important to note that Congress has the ultimate say over those subsidies, not the director of HHS. And, to my mind, it's unlikely that a Republican-controlled congress has sufficient interest in passing subsidies that harm corn farmers who overwhelmingly vote for Republicans.

Finally, RFK's general stance against highly processed foods is something that Republicans have historically balked at. Thus, it's not obvious to me that this sort of agenda will be sufficiently popular with the Trump base such that it can be enacted in a way meaningfully comparable to the promises which have been made.

Thus, my prediction would be that, while RFK Jr.’s agenda might present some headwinds for Pepsi, these are likely to be more short-term than long-term in nature, and, probably more importantly, the magnitude of their impact is likely to be less than the generally accepted current narrative would have you believe.

GLP-1’s and General Trend toward Greater Health Consciousness …

While there has definitely been a significant uptrend in GLP-1 usage over the last few years, it’s important to remember the scale we’re talking about here. According to this source, only 12% of adults have taken one of these drugs, and only 6% are currently taking them. Furthermore, about 6 out of 10 users of these drugs are taking them primarily for a condition other than weight loss (e.g., heart disease or diabetes), with roughly 4 out of 10 having said weight loss is their primary reason for use. Furthermore, as that article notes, Medicare does not currently cover drugs used for weight loss. This underscores a larger point – namely, that the prohibitively high-cost of these GLP-1’s is preventing their widespread adoption, and presumably their adoption over long periods of time, at least for the foreseeable future. All this to say that, while GLP-1’s might present a headwind at the margins, to my mind, there isn’t anything in the data suggesting they are going to be an existential headwind that is going to cut Pepsi’s sales in half or something of the like.

Furthermore, regarding general trends towards health consciousness, such an explanation would predict that we would see changes in Pepsi’s volumes. However, when we look at the data, it is not so clear. Here are the revenue data for PBNA and FLNA regarding the relative contributions of volume and pricing to total revenue growth over the past 10 years:

First, while the beverages segment has seen declines, prior to 2021, these declines, while persistent, had not been outstanding. Moreover, there was a sharp influx in volumes in 2021. Finally, since 2021, the idea that volume declines were the result of Pepsi’s out-sized pricing increases seems just as plausible of an explanation as that there has been some heightened trend of late (e.g., compared to prior to 2021) towards greater health consciousness.

Additionally, and more importantly for the company, since FLNA drives their profitability, there has been very little in the way of volume declines in the FLNA division (in fact, just the opposite up until 2021 before the influx of inflation). The declines that there have been have only really come in the last couple of years, and, as mentioned with the PBNA division, to my mind, these are just as likely the result of out-sized price increases as they were the result of some heightened trend towards healthier lifestyles.

Product recalls, tariffs, supply chain costs, etc.

When you have as many brands as Pepsi has and when you deal in the volume the company does, you’re likely to run into the need for recalls from time to time, unfortunately. Personally, I don’t see these as any more than a blip for the company that they will recover from in the long-term, thanks in large part to the trust and goodwill they’ve been able to engender in the public mind regarding their brands.

And, regarding tariffs, increased supply chain costs, etc., while these are a real thing for Pepsi’s business, they are a real thing for businesses generally. From a competitive perspective, as the previous discussion regarding volumes and pricing alluded to, Pepsi has been able to consistently raise prices year over year, and consumers have largely tolerated it. Sure, perhaps consumers have revolted over the past couple of years in response to some of Pepsi’s out-sized price hikes, but the company is likely simply learning where the limits of its pricing power stands. The point is, though, that the pricing power a company like Pepsi has is the envy of almost any company you could imagine – i.e., they’re about as well-positioned for headwinds like tariffs, supply-chain disruptions, etc., as you could reasonably ask for.

Valuation & Investor Takeaway

So where does this put us? Well, my thinking is that, if you’re at all sympathetic to the sort of counter-arguments I’ve offered here to the prevailing narratives that are out there regarding Pepsi, and if you have ever had any inclination to buy Pepsi’s stock, now is about as good of a time as any to buy. This is underscored by the starting dividend yield being at historically low levels, the company having the history and status of being a dividend king and all that entails (including its demonstrated ability to persist through the ebbs and flows of its sales volumes over time), and the wonderful economics the business enjoys primarily buoyed by the goodwill its brands generally have in consumers’ minds.

Here are a couple of back-of-the-napkin-style valuations that offer some insight into the kind of upside I think new investors are looking at, given current prices (again, ~$131/share at the time of writing).

First, assuming a 3% EPS growth rate and a 20x terminal multiple, I get a stock price of about $183 in 5 years. When you factor in dividend payments, this gets you close to a 50% total return. And I would underscore how conservative these assumptions are - a 20x terminal multiple is definitely on the low-end of where PEP has traded historically, and a 3% EPS growth rate is essentially keeping up with inflation, and, again, is assuming a dramatic drop-off from history for the company.

Second, on the optimistic side, if you grow EPS at about 6% (roughly in-line with historical trends) and assume a 25x terminal multiple (in the mid-to-higher-end of the historical range), I get a stock price of ~$264 in 5 years, for over a ~100% return, and this is to say nothing of the dividends collected along the way.

And really, based on any valuation method of your choosing, I think it's relatively easy to make the case that Pepsi’s stock could provide the sort of 50-100% total return numbers just mentioned over the next roughly 3–5 years. What's more, I think this means the company is well-positioned to out-perform the S&P 500 over this period. Thus, my recommendation is ‘buy.’

data.title: PBDC: Recent Sell-Off Creates An Opportunity
data.description:
Fear often creates opportunities. While higher levels of volatility often make investing more challenging, an uncertain economic environment is usually when most individuals will find the best value.

While investing in the benchmarks has been the best strategy for most of the last decade, business development corporations have also done very well, in particular since 2021 as rates have been going up, but these companies have still been performing well.

One newer fund that focuses on BDC's is the Putnam BDC Income ETF (NYSEARCA:PBDC). This ETF's inception date was in September 2022.

The Putnam BDC Income ETF has offered investors total returns of 60.89 percent since September 2022, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 64.48 percent during this same time period.

PBDC has also consistently outperformed the fund's peers as well.

Today, I am initiating coverage of the Putnam BDC Income fund with a buy rating. This ETF targets BDCs that invest in higher-grade and more liquid companies that should hold up well in the current economic environment. The Fed is also still remaining cautious, rates are not likely to come down significantly, and this investment should continue to be able to pay consistent and impressive income. The recent sell-off creates an opportunity.

PBDC has a listed expense ratio of 13.94 percent, a trailing yield of 10.3 percent, and nearly $192 million in assets under management. The expense ratio is misleading because this includes management fees of individual BDCs. The real expense ratio is close to .7 percent. PBDC's largest holdings are Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), Blackstone (BX), Ares Capital (ARCC), and Golub Capital (GBDC). These positions make up nearly 40 percent of the overall assets of the ETF.

BDCs primarily lend money to smaller and struggling businesses, these asset management firms tend to do best when rates are slightly elevated, but not so high that the solvency of the businesses these companies invest in is at risk.

PBDC specifically targets stronger quality companies with lower leverage and higher amounts of liquidity. The fund's holdings have performed well since this ETF's inception in late 2022. PBDC's top core positions and overall portfolio have continued to outperform in the current more challenging economic environment as well.

Ares Capital recently reported solid net investment income for the first quarter, while Blue Owl Capital's results showed a strong performance across the fund's investment portfolio as well. The Blackstone Secured Lending Fund Ordinary Shares also reported impressive results, including stating that new investments have reached their highest level since 2021. Golub Capital also recently reported that net investment income remained consistent in the second fiscal quarter as well.

PBDC has paid out impressive income over the last 3 years, and the fund's strategy of targeting more liquid and lower leveraged BDC's should offer investors more protection if growth continues to slow as well. PBDC invests primarily in companies that are lending to above-average market cap companies as well.

PBDC has had a year-end yield of over 9 percent since 2023.

Business Development Companies tend to do best in a stable growth environment when rates are slightly elevated, since many of the loans to companies are priced at floating rates. Still, if costs rise too much and rates increase dramatically, that kind of more volatile environment can create solvency issues. While rates are likely to fall in coming months as inflation is down, the Fed remains cautious and overall price levels remain high. The latest job report showed new hiring remained strong, and the IMF is forecasting the US economy to grow at 1.8 percent this year. The economy also remains stable despite growth slowing, and President Trump has indicated that he plans to use his tariffs to negotiate new trade agreements, he doesn't see his recent action as a long-term strategy. The operating environment for most BDCs should remain favorable.

While traditional investing strategies that targeted the benchmarks worked very well over most of the last 10 years, the S&P 500 isn't likely to offer investors the same returns moving forward, more narrowly tailored financial strategies that focus on alternative investments such as business development corporations should offer more value looking ahead. With rates likely to remain somewhat elevated with overall prices still high, and the fundamentals of the economy remaining strong, the sell-off in PBDC creates what should be a good opportunity for income and some growth investors.

data.title: Vornado Takes A Bite Out Of The Big Apple, As Demand For Space Returns
data.description:
A Key NYC REIT Filling The Demand For Space, But Struggling With Margins

With its most recent earnings results on May 5th still fresh and having beat estimates by 12 cents, Vornado Realty Trust (NYSE:VNO) will be the focus of my article today as I take a look at the niche of office REITs.

To get a sense of what this REIT has been up to lately, according to its company website and presentation, it has been active in developing the Penn District of Manhattan, to the tune of millions of square feet in the heart of New York City:

After my research today into 6 different categories, I determined to take a neutral approach and call this stock a Hold.

The following table shows how I got to my score of 6.57 (on a scale of 1 to 10), and the factors that contributed:

I gave greater weight to top-line factors and sector/macro factors I thought will drive future upside, and that is portfolio/pipeline growth and market demand for office and retail in NYC. My future price target also expects a 3-year positive upside.

Where there was weakness was in the profit margin, which can hurt earnings growth, an elevated debt/equity ratio, and disappointing dividend growth trends so far.

Favorable Macro Factors Such as Demand For Office And Retail in NYC, And New Population

In this section, I will focus on the sector and macro environment that impacts this REIT.

Its niche is office and retail as opposed to medical or mortgage REITs, and its "portfolio is concentrated in the nation’s key market — New York City — along with premier assets in both Chicago and San Francisco," according to its corporate website.

So, naturally, I think we should consider what kind of demand there will be for office and retail in those urban markets, particularly NYC.

As someone who worked remote during the 2020 pandemic and afterwards, I still remember the dozens of empty/closed office buildings, not to mention the various stores at the mall that had to close or go out of business completely.

Since then, however, there is a changing trend. In a January article, CNBC pointed to this change:

Demand for New York City office space has finally returned to pre-pandemic levels, driven by an influx of new workers as well as a drive by employers to see current workers return to the office.

Further, in late December, the New York Post also highlighted this trend:

It’s happening all over Midtown. Available floors quickly are gobbled up before data-trackers even know about it, not only at newly constructed “trophy” towers but at sound older locations as well.

I think demand for retail space also is notable, with a January note from real estate firm CBRE Group (CBRE) saying that avg. asking rent in Manhattan's retail corridors is "up 4% year-over-year."

There also is the role of population growth, I think, and how it can impact demand for offices and retail.

From an official City Of New York press release this March, Vornado's primary market of NYC has seen both an influx of people as well as fewer vacant retail properties than in pandemic times:

A recent report found that the city leads the nation in attracting young talent with nearly 500,000 recent college graduates choosing to live in the five boroughs since 2021. The updated census estimate also comes as the city has seen five straight quarters with fewer empty storefronts. The citywide storefront vacancy rate is down to 11 percent, from 30 percent in 2020.

Based on the above evidence, such as recent growth in demand for office and retail space, and an influx of new people, my opinion is that the macro environment looks to be an upside factor for Vornado. It is also evidence that pandemics and remote work do not entirely eliminate the need for offices and stores, but rather just put them on pause for a while.

For this article, I picked just two comparable stocks within the same sector, since I wanted to focus on REITs that have a lot of exposure to NYC and those are: SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG) and Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT).

Positive View Of Top-Line Growth Potential Comes From Portfolio Pipeline, And Tenant List

Next, I will discuss some factors impacting top-line growth potential, with an eye towards the future growth.

Considering the existing nature of Vornado's portfolio, according to its quarterly financial supplement, it is leasing to some big corporate names yet no single tenant in its top 10 makes up more than 8% of annualized rent, something I think reduces the downside risk of overconcentration in a single tenant:

So, one growth factor could be if the above companies do well then they are more likely to keep leasing and paying higher rents. The good news is, its #1 tenant Meta Platforms (META), the Facebook parent company, just "reported stronger-than-expected revenue in the first quarter and provided second-quarter guidance that was in line with Wall Street’s expectations," according to CNBC.

Also on its tenant list is Amazon (AMZN), who recently reported a YoY growth in both net sales and operating income for Q1, according to the company's release.

Another growth factor is growth in the portfolio through acquisitions, and a development pipeline, since I believe a growing portfolio can drive future rental income.

Earlier I showed in the visual that the Penn District is a major development pipeline, with Penn15 and its +2MM sq. ft of leasable space a factor that can positively drive future rental income.

However, one segment I see potential in is need for parking, and I expect some future upside to come from owning parking garages. Vornado in its recent presentation actually showed that its portfolio includes a few thousand sq. ft of parking garages diversified across NYC, Rosslyn (Virginia), and San Francisco (the Mart):

Besides office and parking, from its portfolio we can learn that Vornado has been also developing or has developed space for "70+ new food and beverage offerings," which I think is another potential growth driver beyond just office:

This ties into a personal experience when working once in Manhattan some years ago, as a former NYC-area resident. Although I think many people may run away from the rough winters, congestion, and high cost of living of that area, which is my subjective opinion of course, my old boss used to say that serious companies want to have a Manhattan address, almost like a "trophy" address for prestige.

For instance, his company maintained locations in Florida where work was done, but used a Manhattan office to entertain visiting clients and business partners, and that is where having world-class food/beverage options within walking distance comes into play, not to mention a quick ride to/from major international airports with several daily direct flights to key European business capitals. Thus, despite growth in cities in the south and west, NYC remains a key global financial and business center.

So, I think a bullish case is justified in the category of top-line growth potential, considering Vornado's expanding pipeline across office/retail/food and beverage, expansion beyond NYC to other areas, and a top-tier tenant list without being over-concentrated in a single tenant.

Low Margins And High OpEx Could Impact Bottom Line Growth, While EPS Estimate Negative This Year

Next, I will focus on what could impact the bottom-line, or earnings growth.

I often talk about the importance of strong margins within a sector, so investors may question why Vornado's net income margin is just 3.7%, according to Seeking Alpha data, compared to the sector average of 9.3%.

In addition, its net profit margin is in the middle of the peer group I selected, with ESRT having a much better margin:

I think this data is relevant for investors comparing different office/retail REITs and picking one that is most efficient at turning a profit, that they may want to add to their portfolio.

Considering the trends on the income statement for the quarter ending March, what we can expect going forward, if the trends continue, is that property expenses are a key cost in this type of business, and total OpEx eats up 82% of gross revenues. In some quarters, the firm may get a boost from selling an asset at a gain, and we saw such a gain in Q1, helping to achieve a YoY growth in net income, but those are not each quarter, so there could be lopsided trends.

Rather than just telling, the following visual "shows" the impact to operating income from OpEx, leaving the firm with just $82MM in operating income for the quarter:

In terms of selling assets at a gain, it did so in Jan. 2025 (Q1) and according to its Q1 results commentary "The net proceeds were used to partially redeem Vornado’s preferred equity on the asset."

So, I am leaning less bullish in terms of bottom-line growth potential, due to the weaker margins, the large impact to operating income, and the sporadic nature of asset gains/losses impacting earnings.

Also, I would mention the consensus is calling for an EPS decline this year actually, with a return again the next few years, so I think that data may cause many investors and analysts to be hesitant in buying.

Future Balance Sheet Risk Impacted By High Debt/Equity, And New Borrowing Recently

Next, let's consider some factors impacting balance sheet risk.

For one, debt to equity is a key factor I track, as it gives me a sense of a firm's leverage in relation to its equity.

Compared to the 2 peers I picked, Vornado has the highest D/E ratio of all, and has actually gone up in this 3 year period showing:

From the angle of credit rating agencies, according to the company website, 3 major agencies gave this firm ratings that are sort of on the border of investment and non-investment grade:

In the most recent rating note I found from Fitch, for instance, which was back in Aug. 2024, they seemed to share my concern about rising leverage risk at this firm. In their rating note, they said:

Fitch expects that (re)development costs in the next two years on the Penn District projects, combined with projected near-term occupancy headwinds, will cause VNO's leverage to increase to around the mid-8x level in 2024 before coming down to the mid-7x level in 2025.

Although the firm repaid $450MM in senior unsecured notes in January, according to Q1 results, one of their joint ventures took on $450MM in new financing as follows:

On April 14, 2025, the Fifth Avenue and Times Square JV completed a $450,000,000 financing of 1535 Broadway. The interest only non-recourse loan bears interest at a fixed rate of 6.90% and matures in May 2030.

Thus, investors should think about how the role of interest expenses on such loans can also impact earnings during this time, and then the future debt maturity payment.

Due to the credit ratings being not exactly investment-grade but close, and a higher D/E vs two similar peers, I am more cautious than bullish in the category of balance sheet risk.

Hoping For More Positive Dividend Growth Trends in Future

With dividend payments being a key benefit of owning shares in REITs, as I've said in many articles, particularly since IRS rules require that 90% of a REIT's earnings be paid back to shareholders via dividends, in this section let's focus on a dividend topic of interest.

What really got my attention and not in a good way was that Seeking Alpha gave this stock an "F" grade in terms of 3/5/10yr dividend growth CAGR. For example, its 10yr dividend CAGR is -10%, though the sector average is +2%.

Though its two peers, SLG and ESRT, also had negative CAGR, it was not as bad as Vornado.

Consider the following trends I highlighted below in the dividend growth chart, showing a decline since 2019:

I think this may be an issue of concern to many investors and other analysts, but that is just my opinion, since some investors may not care about dividends at all.

Of those that do, I think we should consider what could drive future growth of dividends and not just historic results, and I think it can be strong operating cashflow, because dividends are paid from cashflow. Unfortunately, Seeking Alpha data shows an expected fwd operating cashflow growth of -17%, even though the sector avg is nearly +5%.

For these reasons, I am not bullish when it comes to dividend growth potential.

Valuation Driving Positive 3-Year Forecast on Share Price

In this final rating category, I hope to merge the discussion of valuation and price targets as it has worked out well in recent articles I published, and showing the relationship between the two.

This being a REIT, the valuation metric of choice, and a forward-looking one, would be the fwd P/FFO (price to funds from operations). In this case, it is a multiple of 16.94x, vs the sector average of 12.67.

I believe the market is reacting bullishly after the firm's Q1 results which show YoY growth in top-line revenue, net income, and operating income, as seen on the income statement too.

So, as of this article writing, this market momentum has driven the shares up to around their 200-day moving average:

As far as they could continue climbing up past that average, I created the following price targets table (using FFO consensus estimates x fwd P/FFO = price target):

From my forecast above, for this fiscal year there could be some downside however in the next two years I expect share price to grow, with a 3 year avg gain of 5%.

This presents a modest case for bullishness, then, in the category of share price upside potential.

I like to compare, however, my price target with that of at least one major financial firm. Weeks before Vornado's Q1 results came out, it seems Piper Sandler (PIPR) lowered the price target to $38, and also mentioned "analysts projecting a challenging year ahead for net income."

Piper's price target is somewhere in between my year 1 and year 2 price target ($36.93 - $40.49), so I am not too far off the mark from a major financial firm's target then.

Conclusion: Agree With Seeking Alpha Quant System Rating, Hold

To conclude, my neutral sentiment today agrees with the Seeking Alpha quant system, which gave a Hold rating to this stock.

In this article, I showed why I think its best qualities are market demand and portfolio growth, as well as future price targets creating upside. However, it shows weaker qualities in terms of margins, dividend growth potential, and balance sheet risk.

A challenge to my neutral rating, to the upside, could be that profit margins greatly improve at this REIT in the next few quarters, and the firm continues beating EPS estimates, making my outlook overly cautious.

A challenge on the downside, though, could be that a key tenant of its runs into financial difficulty or even bankruptcy, posing a risk to rental income and making my outlook overly optimistic. However, what offsets this risk is portfolio diversification, as I mentioned earlier, and no overconcentration in a single tenant driving more than 8% of annual rent.

As I have many readers who are REIT investors, I welcome your commentary on what you think about the outlook for NYC and San Francisco real estate, and what else can be an impacting factor beyond what was said in this article?

Rating Methodology

Albert Anthony is The Analyst, providing commentary on the 2025 financial markets, uncovering fundamental elements from financial statements and ratios, company reports, and macro trends, with a goal of determining the likelihood of a stock's future performance and why.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit hartfordfunds.com.

Share Class Inception: A, I, R3, R4, R5, Y - 9/30/11; F - 2/28/17. Performance shown prior to the inception of a class reflects performance and operating expenses of another class(es) (excluding sales charges, if applicable). Had fees and expenses of a class been reflected for the periods prior to the inception of that class, performance would be different. Since inception (SI) performance is from 9/30/11. Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge.

Performance for periods of less than one year is not annualized.

Benchmark: ICE BofA 1-3 Year BB-B US Cash Pay High Yield Index is a subset of ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index including all securities with a remaining term to final maturity less than 3 years and rated BB1 through B3.

Percentage Of Portfolio

28.89

Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

US fixed-income markets generated positive total returns during the first quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index. Trade tensions and monetary-policy divergence drove market volatility and an increasingly clouded economic outlook. Most fixed-income sectors underperformed US Treasuries as spreads widened amid an expected deteriorating growth/inflation tradeoff.

Performance Summary

The Hartford Low Duration High Income Fund (I Share) underperformed the ICE BofA 1-3 year BB-B US Cash Pay High Yield Index during the fourth quarter.

The Fund’s out-of-benchmark allocations to bank loans and investment-grade corporates detracted from returns over the period.

The Fund’s underweight to and security selection within high-yield credit was a detractor from returns as the sector outperformed duration*-equivalent Treasuries.

The Fund’s out-of-benchmark allocations to the securitized sectors were contributors to performance over the period.

The Fund maintained an overweight to the belly of the yield curve over the period, which contributed to returns as yields fell.

Positioning & Outlook

We continue to be long in our risk posture. All-in yields are attractive and our team’s investment philosophy to emphasize income results in a procyclical tilt in portfolios. We are actively looking to increase our income advantage relative to benchmarks. Uncertainty remains on tariff implementation, resulting in a wide range of potential outcomes for the near-term growth and inflation outlooks. Corporate credit spreads have widened off of their tights, and we continue to find value in areas of high quality securitized. Spreads could remain volatile as macro uncertainty persists.

We remain positive on securitized. Residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) valuations are mixed as some subsectors have tightened more than others. In Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), capital availability for commercial real estate (CRE) should continue to improve, though we are monitoring economic-growth impacts from tariffs as CRE is tied to the health of the economy. In collateralized loan obligations (CLO), loan fundamentals have been resilient amid higher earnings. Expect muted defaults, though slightly elevated when including distressed exchanges. CLO structures are strong.

Investment-grade credit metrics remain healthy, and ratings momentum continues very strong. Forward-looking earnings expectations have begun to roll over, and the negative growth effects of tariffs may accelerate this trend. Spreads are generally trading tight to historical medians and are not fully pricing in the elevated risk of a slowdown in growth and credit conditions over the next 6-12 months. Though, all-in corporate yields remain attractive.

At March-end, high-yield spreads were inside the historical median, though absolute yields were above average. We are cautious on fundamentals; we believe the distribution of macroeconomic outcomes has increased with the new tariffs and US administration policies.

We remain neutral on the bank-loan space. Loan-issuer fundamental trends are declining from a strong position. Revenue growth has slowed but remains positive, and margins and cash flows have been resilient.

Footnote

  1. Effective 3/1/24, the Fund (formerly known as the Hartford Floating Rate High Income Fund) changed its name, objective, principal investment strategy, portfolio managers and benchmark as well as reduced the Fund's contractual management fee.

1Expenses are from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication. Gross expenses do not reflect contractual expense reimbursement arrangements. Net expenses reflect such arrangements in instances when they reduce gross expenses. These arrangements remain in effect until 2/28/26 unless the Fund's Board of Directors approves an earlier termination. Without these arrangements, performance would have been lower.

* Other share classes may have different ratings. The Morningstar RatingTM for funds, or "star rating", is calculated for funds and separate accounts with at least a 3-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. Star rating based on a Morningstar Risk Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance (without adjusting for any sales load, if applicable), placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. 5 stars are assigned to the top 10%, 4 stars to the next 22.5%, 3 stars to the next 35%, 2 stars to the next 22.5%, and 1 star to the bottom 10%. Overall Morningstar Rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. For more information about the Morningstar Fund Ratings, including their methodology, please go to global.morningstar.com/managerdisclosures. ©2025 Morningstar, Inc. All rights reserved. The information contained herein: (1) is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers; (2) may not be copied or distributed; and (3) is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information.

Important Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Security prices fluctuate in value depending on general market and economic conditions and the prospects of individual companies. • Investments in high-yield ("junk") bonds involve greater risk of price volatility, illiquidity, and default than higher-rated debt securities. • The risks associated with mortgage-related and asset-backed securities as well as collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) include credit, interest-rate, prepayment, liquidity, default and extension risk. There are additional risks associated with credit risk transfer securities. • Loans can be difficult to value and less liquid than other types of debt instruments; they are also subject to nonpayment, collateral, bankruptcy, default, extension, prepayment and insolvency risks. • Fixed income security risks include credit, liquidity, call, duration, event and interest-rate risk. As interest rates rise, bond prices generally fall. • Derivatives are generally more volatile and sensitive to changes in market or economic conditions than other securities; their risks include currency, leverage, liquidity, index, pricing, valuation, and counterparty risk. • Foreign investments may be more volatile and less liquid than U.S. investments and are subject to the risk of currency fluctuations and adverse political, economic and regulatory developments. • Restricted securities may be more difficult to sell and price than other securities. • The Fund's investments may fluctuate in value over a short period of time. • The portfolio managers may allocate a portion of the Fund's assets to specialist portfolio managers, which may not work as intended. • Obligations of U.S. Government agencies are supported by varying degrees of credit but are generally not backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. Government. • The Fund may have high portfolio turnover, which could increase its transaction costs and an investor's tax liability.

Investors should carefully consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. This and other important information is contained in a fund's full prospectus and summary prospectus, which can be obtained by visiting hartfordfunds.com. Please read it carefully before investing.

Mutual funds are distributed by Hartford Funds Distributors, LLC (HFD), Member FINRA. Advisory services are provided by Hartford Funds Management Company, LLC (HFMC). Certain funds are sub-advised by Wellington Management Company LLP. HFMC and Wellington Management are SEC registered investment advisers. HFD and HFMC are not affiliated with any sub-adviser.

NOT FDIC INSURED • MAY LOSE VALUE • NO BANK GUARANTEE

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CVAR: Why You Should Avoid This High Fee Active All-Cap Value ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

With only a 12.15% total return since its launch on December 22, 2021, it's safe to say the actively managed Cultivar ETF (BATS:CVAR) has been a bust. Benchmarked against the Russell 3000 Index but designed to appeal to all-cap value investors, CVAR has also lagged behind most passive funds, and my analysis of its current fundamentals indicates that it's likely not to change moving forward. As a result, I've assigned a "sell" rating on CVAR, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

CVAR Overview

Strategy Discussion

CVAR is an actively managed fund that launched on December 22, 2021. As of May 5, 2025, it has 90 holdings, a 0.87% expense ratio, $31.50 million in assets under management, and is benchmarked against the Russell 3000 Index. As shown below, it's trailed that benchmark by 1.80% per year (4.40% vs. 6.20%) since its inception. Notably, the performance gap was 5.49% over the last three years as of March 31, 2025, though it has demonstrated solid gains in Q1.

CVAR's principal investment strategies, as described in the fund prospectus, note how the portfolio is comprised of "approximately 50 to 100 securities across a broad spectrum of market capitalizations and sectors." Furthermore, the strategy is value-oriented, which aligns with the categories assigned by third-party data providers like Morningstar. As shown below, CVAR's style has consistently been "Mid Value" since its inception, while its category changed from "US Fund Large Value" in 2021 to "US Fund Mid-Cap Value" thereafter.

To achieve its investment objective, the prospectus states the following:

The Fund invests primarily in equity securities traded on U.S. exchanges of any size that the Fund's investment adviser, Cultivar Capital, Inc. (the "Adviser") believes are under-valued or under-appreciated by other participants in the broader equity market. In selecting securities for the Fund, the Adviser screens on an ongoing basis the Fund's investment universe seeking such securities using an initial valuation assessment that is primarily quantitative, based on cash flow. The Adviser's discounted cash flow model uses several key inputs to assess each security's estimated intrinsic value. The key inputs include a conservative annual cash flow assumption, a desired rate of return, and an average growth rate for the security as implied by the market historically.

The prospectus also states that the Adviser aims for a 20-40% discount to their calculated intrinsic value and that common stocks, ADRs, and REITs are all eligible securities. Securities trading at sufficient discounts go through a "multi-layered review within the Adviser's portfolio management team," which involves assessing the security's relative risk and uncertainty of its future cash flows. Lastly, the Adviser gives consideration to "top-down, macro valuation factors, including sector diversification," so it sounds pretty comprehensive.

However, the strategy also hasn't worked well so far, and I'll try to shed some light on why in the fundamental analysis section. But first, let's take a closer look at CVAR's performance track record against the iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV) and three all-cap value funds: the WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund ETF (WTV), the iShares Focused Value Factor ETF (FOVL), and the Vanguard U.S. Value Factor ETF (VFVA). These ETFs have lower expense ratios than CVAR and are also more established, though WTV's strategy changed on December 18, 2017, so I caution against reading too much into its results prior to that date.

Performance Summary

Since its launch on December 22, 2021, CVAR has gained 12.15% compared to 22.19%, 38.43%, 32.94%, and 15.33% for IWV, WTV, FOVL, and VFVA, respectively. We knew from earlier that CVAR had trailed its benchmark, but it's disappointing to see it also lag behind other funds in the same category. It's also performed poorly compared to value ETFs, regardless of size. For example, its 7.11% three-year total return as of April 30, 2025, ranks #120/139 among all the U.S. value ETFs I track.

The next table from Portfolio Visualizer highlights some additional risk and return metrics for the five ETFs since January 2022. One positive feature is that CVAR seems to manage downside risk better than its peers, evidenced by a relatively low 17.07% maximum drawdown and -5.03% "worst year" figure, which occurred in 2022. Furthermore, the earlier table from Morningstar shows how CVAR is only 88.79% invested in equities, with the remaining in Treasury Bonds and Cash / Cash Equivalents. That's more than from 2021-2024, so it appears the firm's outlook for equities is diminishing.

CVAR Analysis

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

The next table highlights the sector allocation differences between CVAR, IWV, WTV, FOVL, and VFVA. Please note, these allocations are only for the equity portion of the portfolio.

We see CVAR underweights the Financials sector by 19.46% on average, though the number is smaller if you were to exclude FOVL, which allocates 55.41% to that sector. CVAR is heavy in Consumer Staples at 13.01%, which aligns well with the firm's seemingly cautious outlook. Still, the good sector diversification, as described in the fund's prospectus, is evident. Every sector is represented, and no sector exceeds 20% of the portfolio.

CVAR Fundamentals: Top 25 Holdings

Next, please consider the following fundamental analysis for CVAR's top 25 equity holdings, which comprise 54.41% of the portfolio on a cash-adjusted basis. I've also included summary statistics for IWV, WTV, FOVL, and VFVA at the bottom to help us identify CVAR's strengths and weaknesses.

Here are three observations to consider:

1. CVAR has a 0.85 five-year portfolio beta, which indicates it's less risky than the broader market. I calculated this beta using the weighted average betas of CVAR's current holdings, and as we see below, CVAR's one-year beta (based on the ETF's prior holdings) has consistently been below average, now at only 0.469. This tells me managers have been conservative for a while, and that's why they've missed out on the market's big run-up after 2022.

2. CVAR trades at 15.02x forward earnings and 10.34x trailing cash flow, which is quite elevated compared to the three value ETFs listed. In my view, WTV is a far superior option. Its forward P/E ratio is just 12.55x, it features better historical and estimated sales and earnings growth rates, and it's also less risky than the market, evidenced by its 0.96 five-year beta. FOVL is even cheaper, but that's because it's allocated over 55% to Financials, and I think that concentration in any sector is an added risk itself. As for VFVA, it's also cheap, but the growth isn't there, with analysts expecting just a 4.77% increase in earnings per share over the next twelve months.

One interesting statistic is that CVAR's holdings have seen their share prices decline by 4.38% over the last year, compared to +14.29% and +11.15% for WTV and FOVL. This could be caused by managers not adjusting their future cash flow assumptions when warranted, indicating they believe such price drops are only temporary. That could be the case, but what they might not factor in is that CVAR's holdings have negative earnings outlooks among Wall Street analysts. I calculated a 4.72/10 EPS Revision Score using Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, which ranks #168/190 among all the U.S. value ETFs I track. Furthermore, CVAR's holdings have done nothing over the last three and five years, declining by 23.24% and 9.99%, respectively. Intentional or not, managers essentially want to convince you that this is the moment they'll revert back to their fair value, which, I think, is a big stretch.

3. Another reason why CVAR's selections have performed poorly is because many are low quality. As shown in the first table, the portfolio's weighted average ROA and ROE figures are only 5.51% and 12.82%, respectively, compared to 8.02% and 22.01% for WTV, my preferred choice in this group. To account for sector composition differences between the five funds, I also calculated sector-adjusted profit scores, and CVAR's 7.76/10 is below WTV's, albeit better than FOVL and VFVA. In short, there are just too many reasons not to select WTV over CVAR, as WTV has:

  • a lower expense ratio (0.12% vs. 0.87%)
  • a stronger track record (38.40% vs. 12.15% since CVAR's inception)
  • better sector-adjusted scores on all factors except value (growth, momentum, quality, earnings revisions)

Investment Recommendation

I've assigned a "sell" rating to CVAR due to its poor track record, high expense ratio, and weak overall fundamentals. I was unable to assess the inputs of its proprietary cash-flow-based model, but the output is clearly contrarian, as the current portfolio consists primarily of stocks that have generated negative returns over the last one, three, and five years. I suspect the likely cause is a reluctance to adjust a company's cash flow assumptions as new information becomes available, which could include declining profit margins or negative consensus Wall Street earnings revisions. Either way, WTV looks superior, and I would suggest that as an alternative for all-cap value investors.
